original_abstract: Solar cells fabricated using alkyl ammonium metal halides as light absorbers have the right combination of high power conversion efficiency and ease of fabrication to realize inexpensive but efficient thin film solar cells. However, they degrade under prolonged exposure to sunlight. Herein, we show that this degradation is quasi-reversible, and that it can be greatly lessened by simple modifications of the solar cell operating conditions. We studied perovskite devices using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with methylammonium (MA)-, formamidinium (FA)-, and MA(x)FA(1-x) lead triiodide as active layers. From variable temperature EIS studies, we found that the diffusion coefficient using MA ions was greater than when using FA ions. Structural studies using powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) show that for MAPbI3 a structural change and lattice expansion occurs at device operating temperatures. On the basis of EIS and PXRD studies, we postulate that in MAPbI3 the predominant mechanism of accelerated device degradation under sunlight involves thermally activated fast ion transport coupled with a lattice-expanding phase transition, both of which are facilitated by absorption of the infrared component of the solar spectrum. Using these findings, we show that the devices show greatly improved operation lifetimes and stability under white-light emitting diodes, or under a solar simulator with an infrared cutoff filter or with cooling.
humanized_abstract: Solar cells based on alkyl ammonium metal halides as photoactive materials have recently shown promising combination of high power conversion efficiency and facile fabrication, pointing towards low-cost thin film solar cells. However, the stability of these solar cells has been a matter of concern and they show significant degradation under sunlight exposure. Here, we report that this degradation is almost quasi-reversible and could be highly suppressed by some minor adjustments in operating conditions of solar cells. The perovskite solar cells using methylammonium (MA)-, formamidinium (FA)-, and mixture of MA(x)FA(1-x) lead triiodide as active material were characterized using the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The variable temperature EIS has shown that the diffusion coefficient of MA ions is much higher than that of FA ions. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) has also confirmed structural change and lattice expansion in MAPbI3 upon heating to operating temperatures. Based on our EIS and PXRD results, we inferred that the enhanced ion movement (fast ion transport) in MAPbI3 under solar light illumination that triggers the thermally activated lattice-expanding phase transition. This effect is attributed to absorption of infrared component of the sun light. Hence, stable solar cell operation lifetimes and under white-light emitting diodes, or under a solar simulator with an infrared cutoff filter or cooling conditions.
